title: Dart Language
objectives: Understand Dart via a real-life analogy
Master null safety, records, patterns, and pattern-matching switch
Use Futures, async/await, and Streams idiomatically
Recognize what makes Dart good for Flutter (AOT + JIT, isolates)

sections.markdown:
**Think of Dart as the engine of a hybrid car.**

- **In development (`flutter run`)** — the JIT engine spins up instantly. Hot reload works because the code is being interpreted + JITed live. You edit, save, see the change in ~300 ms without losing state. Perfect for iteration.
- **In production (`flutter build apk/aab/ipa/web`)** — an AOT compiler ahead-of-time compiles Dart to native ARM/x64 machine code. The final app boots fast, runs at native speed, and has predictable frame times (no JIT jitter mid-scroll).
- **Same source code, two engines** — the reason Flutter feels *both* fast to develop and fast to ship. It's the single most underrated architectural choice in Flutter.

**And the language itself?** Dart looks like TypeScript that had a serious workout: null-safe types, first-class classes, sealed classes, records, pattern matching, top-level functions, and `async`/`await` that everyone else copied. It's Java-family without the ceremony, JavaScript-family without the surprises.

**Dart** is a null-safe, object-oriented, class-based language from Google (2011). Every value is an object; strong type system with sound null safety since Dart 3 (2023); dual JIT (development / dartpad) + AOT (production Flutter) compilation. Runs on the **Dart VM** (native) or compiles to **JavaScript** (web) and **native machine code** (Flutter mobile/desktop). It's the language Flutter is written IN and Flutter apps are written IN.
## The Dart features you'll actually use every day

rows: Every variable — types tell you if null is possible
final = set-once at runtime; const = compile-time literal
Local variables — let the compiler infer
Return multiple values without a class
Add methods to types you don't own
True parallelism (each isolate has its own heap)

sections.markdown: ## Null safety — types tell you if null is possible
## Records + patterns (Dart 3, 2023)
## Async — Future, Stream, and isolates
## Sample program — a small end-to-end program
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Using `!` (bang) everywhere.** Same as Kotlin's `!!` — tells the compiler 'trust me, not null' and crashes at runtime if you're wrong. Prefer `?.`, `??`, or `if (x != null)` (which smart-narrows the type).
**Mistake 2: `late` when you don't need it.** `late` disables null-safety guarantees for that variable. Only use for framework-injected fields (like a Bloc's `stream` that's set in `initState`).

**Mistake 3: Confusing `final` and `const`.** `final` = reference assigned once at runtime (`final now = DateTime.now()`). `const` = compile-time literal (`const Duration(seconds: 1)`). `const` values are canonicalized (one instance for identical values) — huge for Flutter widget trees.
**Mistake 4: Blocking the event loop.** `for (var i = 0; i < 10000000; i++) …` on the main isolate freezes the UI. Long CPU work → offload with `Isolate.run(...)`.
**Mistake 5: Ignoring cancellation of Streams.** Subscriptions leak if not cancelled. Prefer `StreamSubscription.cancel()` in `dispose()` or auto-managed with Provider/Bloc.
## Best practices
**1. Default to `final` and immutable data.** State that doesn't change can't be wrong.
**2. Prefix widget constants with `const`.** `const Text('Home')` skips rebuilds — one of Flutter's biggest perf wins.
**3. Use records + patterns for one-shot data.** Beats writing a class for every tuple.
**4. Model results as `sealed class Result<T>`.** Ok / Err — exhaustive matching kills the try/catch pyramid.
**5. Isolates for CPU work, async for I/O.** Don't confuse them — most 'my app is slow' problems are I/O on the main isolate, fixed by `await`.
**6. Adopt `analysis_options.yaml` with `flutter_lints`.** Catches null-safety violations, unused imports, and 100+ style issues at edit time.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Null-safe chain.** Given `User? → Address? → City?`, write a one-liner returning the city or 'unknown'.

**Why:** Muscle memory for `?.` + `??`.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Records + destructuring.** Parse a CSV row into `(String name, int age, String? email)` and destructure.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — sealed Result.** Wrap a `Future<Either<Error,User>>` pattern in `Result<T>` with `map` and `fold`.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Stream + async*.** Emit a running average every second from a synthetic sensor stream.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Isolate.run.** Move a 500-ms JSON parse off the main isolate; measure jank before/after with Flutter's perf overlay.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: Why does Flutter use Dart?**

**A:** Three reasons. **(1) Dual compilation** — Dart supports both JIT (fast dev + hot reload) and AOT (fast production startup + predictable frame times). No other mainstream language nails both. **(2) Single-thread event loop with isolates** — matches Flutter's declarative render model perfectly; long CPU work is opt-in to an isolate, not accidentally scattered across threads. **(3) Ergonomics** — Dart is a small, focused language with null safety, records, patterns, sealed classes, async/await, extension methods. Compare to a Flutter-in-JavaScript alternative: no null safety, no sealed classes, worse performance. Google *did* try Flutter on Java (early prototypes) and dropped it. Dart isn't Flutter's constraint — it's Flutter's superpower.

**Q2: `final` vs `const` — difference?**

**A:** **`final`** — the reference is assigned exactly once and then read-only. The value itself may be constructed at runtime: `final now = DateTime.now();`. **`const`** — a compile-time constant. The value must be entirely known at compile time. `const Duration(seconds: 1)`. Two things a `const` object always gets: **canonicalization** (identical values share the same underlying instance in memory — `identical(const Duration(seconds:1), const Duration(seconds:1))` is `true`), and it can be used as a `const` argument or `const` widget subtree. **In Flutter:** prefix as many widget constructors as possible with `const` — Flutter skips rebuilding a `const` widget subtree because it knows its value hasn't changed.

**Q3: How does Dart's null safety work?**

**A:** Nullability is part of the type. `String` is non-nullable; `String?` is nullable. The compiler forbids passing a `String?` where a `String` is expected without checking. Operators: **`?.`** — safe call; **`??`** — null-coalescing default; **`!`** — force non-null (throws if null); **`x != null`** — smart-narrows `x` to non-null inside the if. Dart 3 added **sound** null safety — meaning the guarantees hold even across dynamic dispatch (in older 'unsound' null safety, `dynamic` could smuggle nulls in). **Effect:** whole classes of `NoSuchMethodError: null` bugs go extinct at compile time.

**Q4: `async`/`await` vs `Isolate.run` — when to use which?**

**A:** **`async`/`await`** is single-threaded cooperative concurrency. Your code yields (`await`) at each I/O point and the SAME isolate can run other queued microtasks in the meantime. Perfect for **I/O**: network, file, DB. **`Isolate.run(() => heavy())`** is TRUE parallelism — spawns a fresh isolate with its own heap, runs the work there, returns the result. Perfect for **CPU**: image decode, big JSON parse, ML inference. **Golden rule:** if your CPU is idle but your UI is stuck, you're doing I/O without `await`. If your CPU is 100% but your UI is stuck, you're doing CPU work on the main isolate — offload to `Isolate.run`.

**Q5: What are records and patterns (Dart 3)?**

**A:** **Records** are anonymous, structurally-typed groups of values. `(int, String) pair = (1, 'Ada')`. With named fields: `({int id, String name}) rec = (id: 1, name: 'Ada')`. Great for returning multiple values without writing a class. **Patterns** are declarative shapes you can match and destructure: `final (a, b) = pair;` binds `a=1, b='Ada'`. Combined with pattern-matching `switch`, you can destructure sealed hierarchies exhaustively:
```dart
switch (shape) {
  Circle(:final r) => Math.pi * r * r,
  Square(:final s) => s * s,
}
```
This is Dart's equivalent of Kotlin's data classes + `when`, Rust's enums + `match`, Java 21's records + sealed. Modern Dart code leans on it heavily for domain modeling.

**Q6: What are sealed classes and how do they help?**

**A:** A `sealed class Foo {}` restricts subclassing to the same library. Combined with pattern-matching `switch`, the compiler **exhaustively** checks every case is handled — no `default:` needed, and adding a new subclass forces every switch to update. This gives Dart **algebraic data types**: model a `Result<T>` as `Ok<T>(value)` | `Err(error)`, or a `Shape` as `Circle` | `Square` | `Triangle`. Eliminates the 'forgot a case' bug that plagues open class hierarchies, and replaces visitor-pattern boilerplate. Introduced in Dart 3 (2023); the modern default for domain modeling.

**Q7: What are extension methods?**

**A:** Add methods to a type you don't own. `extension StringX on String { bool get isValidEmail => ... }` — now every `String` in scope has `.isValidEmail`. Under the hood, extensions compile to static functions with the receiver as the first argument; they're syntactic sugar, not open-class monkey-patching. Extensions can't access private members. Widely used in Flutter — `context.theme`, `context.push('/route')`, `50.px`, `String.capitalize()` — for concise, readable domain code. **Prefer extensions to util classes** wherever the receiver is obvious.

**Q8: JIT vs AOT — what do they compile to and when?**

**A:** **JIT (Just-In-Time)** — Dart source is parsed and compiled to bytecode on the fly. `flutter run` uses JIT for the app on the device. The killer feature it enables: **hot reload** (~300 ms round-trip from edit to running change, preserving app state) and **hot restart** (~1s, fresh state). **AOT (Ahead-of-Time)** — `flutter build apk/aab/ipa/web` compiles Dart directly to native ARM/x64 (or JavaScript for web). No VM overhead, no JIT warm-up, predictable performance, small binaries. Same source code — Dart's tooling picks the compiler based on the build target. That's why Flutter feels fast to develop AND fast to ship.

## Real-world usage

- **Every Flutter app** — Dart is the primary language.
- **Server-side (Dart Frog, Shelf, Serverpod)** — same language front-and-back.
- **CLI tools** — `dart run` your own tools; compile to a single-binary with `dart compile exe`.
- **Flutter Web** — Dart compiles to JavaScript (dart2js) or WebAssembly (dart2wasm).
- **Google Ads, Google Pay, BMW MyBMW, Alibaba, TenCent** — mainstream Flutter apps you use.
- **The Flutter SDK itself** — written mostly in Dart.

## Advanced notes

- **`dart:async` internals** — Futures use a microtask queue; nested `await`s don't leak stack frames.
- **`Zone`** — advanced context (uncaught error handling, `runZonedGuarded`, `HttpOverrides`).
- **`Isolate.spawn` vs `Isolate.run`** — `run` is the modern one-shot helper; `spawn` for long-lived isolates with message passing.
- **`SendPort`/`ReceivePort`** — message-passing between isolates. No shared memory — Dart's design keeps it simple.
- **`dart2wasm`** — compiles Dart to WebAssembly for Flutter Web; faster than dart2js in many workloads.
- **`Function.apply`** — call a function reflectively (rare).
- **`dart:mirrors`** — reflection, disabled in Flutter (breaks tree-shaking).
- **`dart compile exe`** — ship a standalone native binary of a Dart CLI.
- **`dart pub` + `pubspec.yaml`** — package manager; hosted at pub.dev.
- **`analysis_options.yaml`** — enforce lints; `include: package:flutter_lints/flutter.yaml`.

flashcards.back: JIT for hot reload + AOT for production speed; single-thread event loop matches declarative UI; strong ergonomics.
`final` = set-once at runtime. `const` = compile-time literal + canonicalized; skip-rebuild in Flutter.
Types encode nullability. `String` never null; `String?` may be. Enforced at compile time.
async/await = cooperative concurrency for I/O (single isolate). Isolate = true parallelism for CPU (separate heaps).
Anonymous grouped values; `(int, String)` or `({int id, String name})`. Destructure with patterns.
Algebraic data types. Compiler proves the switch is exhaustive; no default needed.

quiz.explain: Flutter's biggest perf win — skip rebuilds when the subtree is const.

options: Exhaustive matching over a closed hierarchy

challenges.text: Prefix every possible widget constructor in a screen with `const`.
Model Result<T> as a sealed class with Ok/Err; add `map` + `fold`.
Parse a CSV row to a record `(String, int, String?)` and destructure.
Offload JSON parse of a 1MB file with Isolate.run and measure the delta.
Migrate an unsound-null-safety Dart 2 codebase to Dart 3 sound null safety.
